front: Interview question: Why would you choose Apache Iceberg over plain Parquet files on S3?
back: Plain Parquet on S3 is just a file format with no table semantics — it has none of the features that make a data platform reliable at scale. With Iceberg over Parquet on S3 you get: (1) ACID transactions — concurrent writers don't corrupt each other; plain Parquet has no concurrency control. (2) Schema evolution — rename columns, add fields without breaking downstream readers; plain Parquet requires rewriting all files. (3) Partition evolution — change partitioning strategy without a full data rewrite. (4) Time travel — query any past snapshot for debugging or audit; plain Parquet files are overwritten and gone. (5) Efficient query planning — Iceberg statistics (min/max per column) enable skipping irrelevant files without listing millions of S3 objects; plain Parquet requires expensive S3 LIST operations. (6) Row-level updates/deletes (MERGE INTO, DELETE FROM) — impossible natively with immutable Parquet files. (7) Exactly-once streaming writes — aligned with Flink checkpoints. The cost: slight operational overhead (catalog, maintenance jobs). The benefit: a real, production-grade table that behaves like a database table on cheap object storage.
